response: Enacted in May 2018, the EU’s GDPR fundamentally reshaped data privacy by requiring strict user consent, 72-hour breach reporting, and expansive data rights for any organization processing EU residents' information. Violations carry penalties of up to 4% of global annual revenue or €20 million, resulting in landmark fines against tech giants like Meta (€1.2 billion), Amazon (€746 million), and Google (over €200 million). While critics argue the regulation imposes heavy compliance costs on small businesses, creates user friction, and suffers from inconsistent enforcement, it has successfully established data privacy as a fundamental right. Ultimately, the GDPR has set the global benchmark for data protection, directly inspiring similar privacy laws worldwide.
